description: This figure presents a comprehensive analysis of austenite stability during intercritical annealing (IA) for two medium-Mn steels: M1 (Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C) and M2 (Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C). Subplots (a1) and (a2) display the calculated retained austenite fraction at room temperature (using the Koistinen-Marburger model) and corresponding Ms temperatures as functions of annealing temperature (600–900°C). The shaded regions indicate temperatures where Ms is below room temperature, predicting complete retention of equilibrium austenite. Above a critical intercritical annealing temperature (IAT), the calculated retained austenite decreases significantly: for M1 at 700°C, the calculated fraction is ~16% versus observed ~45%; for M2 at 700°C, calculated ~11% versus observed ~19%. Subplot (b) shows grain size distributions, revealing that both steels contain substantial fractions of fine austenite grains (<0.5 μm), with M1 exhibiting a higher proportion of ultrafine grains than M2, contributing to greater austenite stability. Subplot (c) provides schematic illustrations of elemental partitioning during IA: Mn and C partition from ferrite (α) to austenite (γ) in both steels (red arrows), while M2 additionally shows Si partitioning from austenite to δ-ferrite and α-ferrite (black arrows). The figure supports the conclusion that local microstructural heterogeneities—specifically Mn enrichment due to incomplete diffusion and the stabilizing effect of fine grains (<0.5 μm)—explain the discrepancy between equilibrium-calculated and experimentally observed retained austenite fractions, with Si addition altering the partitioning behavior and reducing austenite stability in M2.
